segments.s586-g49: سواری (a horseman) is the direct noun introduced first in a somewhat inverted, poetic order. برافگند is a past-tense, 3rd-person singular verbal form meaning 'let loose/propelled' (an archaic/perhaps poetic perfect). زی + شهریار forms a prepositional/adverbial phrase indicating direction/target (classical usage places this short preposition before the noun), so the whole segment means 'he set a horseman toward the king.'
segments.s586-g50: فرستاد is a simple past (3rd‑person singular) 'he sent / dispatched.' نزدیک functions as an adverb 'near / close,' and او is the 3rd‑person pronoun 'him/he'; آن نگار is a noun phrase 'that beloved' (آن = 'that' + نگار = 'beloved' or fair one). Word order is subject omitted (understood from context) + verb + adverbial phrase, typical of Persian: 'he sent that beloved near him.'
segments.s586-g51: فرستاده is a noun 'messenger' (agent/person sent). چون here works as a temporal conjunction 'when' (classical/poetic use), and شد is the past auxiliary/verb 'became/arrived' — together forming the temporal clause 'when the messenger arrived.' بر + یزدگرد indicates the target/place 'before/to Yazdegerd' (preposition + proper name).
segments.s586-g52: همه لشکر is the subject 'the whole army.' آمد is the past verb 'came' (3rd‑person singular/plural as required by the epic style). بران نامه گرد is a compact phrase: بران = 'upon/around that,' نامه = 'letter,' and گرد here is a postpositive particle meaning 'around/about' (so 'gathered around that letter'). The order is emphatic/poetic, placing the object phrase after the verb.
segments.s586-g53: همه نامداران is a nominative phrase 'all the renowned men.' فروماندند is a past, 3rd‑person plural verb meaning 'they remained/they were left behind' (an older/perfective form). Together: 'all the worthies remained.'
segments.s586-g54: به بهرام is a prepositional phrase 'to/for Bahram.' بر آفرین is an idiomatic collocation where آفرین = 'acclamation/ bravo' and بر links the acclamation to the addressee; خواندند is past plural 'they uttered/they sang/called out.' The clause therefore means 'they shouted acclamation to Bahram.'
segments.s586-g55: وزان پس is a temporal/adverbial compound: وزان = 'from that' / 'from that time,' and پس = 'then/after.' هنرها is the plural subject 'the arts/skills/talents.' چو کردی is a subordinate conditional/temporal clause: چو = 'when/whenever' and کردی = 'you did/you employed' (2nd‑person past). به کار is a prepositional phrase 'into work / into use' (i.e., 'put to use'). Together: 'and from that time, whenever you used your skills...'.
segments.s586-g56: همی is an archaic adverb prefixed to verbs meaning 'always/continually' and here modifies the past verb تاختندی (3rd‑person plural past of تاختن) 'they rushed/charged.' بر شهریار is the prepositional object 'against the king.' The whole clause expresses habitual or repeated action: 'they would always charge at the king.'
segments.s586-g9: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate time-clause. او is the pronoun 'he' (subject). گور و شیر دلاور are the coordinated objects (gūr = onager, and the brave lion) placed before the verb, and بکشت is the past verb 'slew/struck down' (an archaic form with ب- prefix). The sequence follows an elevated/poetic SOV order with چو + subject + objects + verb.
segments.s586-g10: به ایوان خرامید is a main-clause action: به + noun phrase marks movement 'into/towards the iwan' and خرامید is the past of a stative/iterative verb 'to stride proudly'. تیغی به مشت is an appended nominative/absolute phrase meaning 'a sword in (his) fist' (تیغی indefinite sword), with به marking the instrumental/locative sense 'in/with' مشت; the phrase supplies the instrument and follows the verb for poetic emphasis.
segments.s586-g11: دگر هفته is a temporal phrase 'the next/another week' placed before the subject for topicalization. نعمان و منذر are the coordinated subject names, and به راه is a prepositional phrase 'onto the road/on the way' marking their movement; the preposition به here indicates motion toward.
segments.s586-g12: همی رفت is an imperfective/continuous past construction (همی + past verb) meaning 'were going/kept going' or simply 'went'. با او 'with him' marks accompaniment, and به نخچیرگاه gives the destination 'to the hunting-ground'. The clause is straightforward S + VP with the aspect marker همی before the verb.
segments.s586-g13: بسی here functions as a quantifier 'many/much' (classical usage). نامور modifies the following noun/participle as 'renowned', and برده is a past participle 'taken' (i.e., 'taken/captured'). از تازیان marks the source 'from the Arabs'. The phrase is an NP + participle describing many captives taken from the Arabs.
segments.s586-g14: کزیشان is a fused relative/prepositional phrase meaning 'from them' or 'of whom' (the רelative source), with an implicit copula omitted as is common in epic style. بدی راه literally 'the badness/evil of the way' means 'evil ways' or 'ill conduct', and سود و زیان 'profit and loss' specifies the domain: 'ill ways concerning gain and loss.' The clause is elliptical: 'from them came ill ways in matters of profit and loss.'
segments.s586-g15: همی خواست is the past imperfective or customary past 'wished/desired' with همی giving an ongoing or regular nuance; منذر is the subject 'Munḏir'. که introduces a subordinate clause (object of 'wanted'). بهرام گور is the subject of that subordinate clause (name with the epithet Gūr = 'Onager').
segments.s586-g16: بدیشان is the indirect object 'to them/for them' (ب + ایشان). نماید is the present/subjunctive form 'should show/give/provide' (subordinate optative after خواست که). سواری و زور are the direct objects: 'riders/horsemen (i.e., cavalry) and strength/might.' Word order places the verb before its objects here because it belongs to the subordinate clause.
segments.s586-g33: بدو گفت is a fixed word order meaning 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb). منذر is the speaker's name (subject). که‌ای شهریار is a vocative/direct address: که (here introducing direct speech or exclamation) + ای (O) + شهریار (sovereign).
segments.s586-g34: بتو (به + تو) is a prepositional phrase 'for/to you' placed before the predicate. شادمانم is an adjectival predicate with the first‑person singular suffix -م, 'I am glad/rejoicing'. چو is a comparative/simile 'like/as'. گلبن به بار is the image compared to: گلبن 'rosebud' + به بار ('to bearing' / 'in fruit') — به is part of the idiomatic expression 'به بار آمدن' (to bear/come to fruition) and here links the simile to the idea of fruitful bloom.
segments.s586-g35: مبادا is an optative/negative particle 'may it not be' / 'lest'. که introduces the subordinate clause. خم آورد is a poetic verbal phrase 'to cause to bend/stoop' (خم = bend; آورد is the classical third‑person verbal element), so here 'that [the] moon be bent'. ماه تو is the metaphorical 'your moon' (= your glory/face).
segments.s586-g36: وگر is a conditional 'but if/and if'. سست گردد contains the adjective سست 'weak' plus the verb گردد (گشت/گرد) 'become' in a classical/poetic present/subjunctive form, rendering 'should become weak'. کمرگاه تو 'your kamar‑gāh' = 'your waist/loin' is the patient of that condition.
segments.s586-g37: هم‌انگه ('at that moment') is an adverbial temporal phrase. چون here means 'as/when' introducing a temporal clause. منذر is the subject; به ایوان رسید is a past tense clause: به + ایوان 'to the iwan/portico' and رسید (past of رسیدن) 'reached/arrived'.
segments.s586-g38: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' indicating source. بهرام is the name (source of the report). رایش means 'his judgment/opinion/report' (رای + third‑person marker). به کیوان رسید is 'reached Keyvān' (به + proper name + رسید), with رسید again simple past 'arrived/reached'.
segments.s586-g39: فراوان is an adverb/adjective 'numerous/plentiful'. مصور is a noun 'painter/illustrator' (here plurality is implied by context). بجست is an archaic/poetic past form 'sprang up/leapt forth'. از یمن gives the origin/source 'from Yaman' (here poetic geography/point of origin).
segments.s586-g40: شدند is the past third‑person plural of شدن used in the sense 'they came to be / they gathered'. این سران 'these chiefs' is the subject. بر درش is a prepositional phrase 'upon/at his door' where ش is the third‑person possessive suffix 'his'. انجمن is the predicate noun 'assembly/meeting' — together: 'these chiefs formed an assembly at his door.'
segments.s586-g17: Word order is subject-verb-object/locative: the plural past verb دیدند ('they saw') follows the noun شترمرغ ('ostrich') and is accompanied by the locative جایی ('somewhere'). گله is the noun 'flock/herd' serving as the object/complement describing what they saw. The simple past form دیدند marks a completed action by a plural subject.
segments.s586-g18: دوان is a concise verbal form meaning 'ran/ran off' or 'rushed' (here a past or narrative action). هر یکی ('each one') is a distributive phrase (هر + یکی) meaning 'each one'. چون introduces a simile 'like/as', and هیونی یله is a descriptive phrase: هیونی = 'beast/animal' with the adjectival ending -ی, and یله = 'loose/unrestrained', so together 'like a loose/wild beast'.
segments.s586-g19: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. The proper name بهرام‌گور is the subject, آن شترمرغ is the definite object 'that ostrich', and دید is the past-tense verb 'saw/beheld'. Word order is classical SOV in the clause (subject — object — verb).
segments.s586-g20: به کردار is a prepositional phrase 'in the manner/like' (به + noun), where کردار here functions as 'manner/behavior'. باد هوا is a poetic collocation 'wind-air' meaning 'like the wind' or 'as if borne by the air'. بردمید is a past transitive verb 'took/seized up'; together the phrase says he seized it up as the wind would (i.e., quickly or by a gust).
segments.s586-g21: کمان را uses the object marker را to mark کمان ('the bow') as definite object. بمالید is a past transitive verb literally 'rubbed/handled' here meaning he took or manipulated the bow; خندان is a participle/adjective 'smiling' describing the subject's manner, and به + چنگ ('in/by a claw/hand') indicates the instrument/way he held it. The preposition به is used adverbially 'with/in'.
segments.s586-g22: بزد is a simple past 'he struck/hit'. بر کمر is a locative phrase 'upon the waist/flank' (بر = on/upon + کمر = waist/flank). چار تیر خدنگ is a noun group 'four dagger-like arrows' with the numeral چار preceding تیر and خدنگ functioning as an adjectival descriptor ('dagger(-like)'). The whole clause states the target and the manner of the blow.
segments.s586-g23: یکایک is an adverbial distributive 'one by one / each and every'. همی is a classical particle often used in narrative for continuous or emphatic aspect (here giving a narrative/ongoing sense). راند is the past tense 'he drove/placed/nocked', and اندر کمان is the locative 'into/in the bow' (اندر = in/within). Together: 'he one-by-one placed (them) into the bow.'
segments.s586-g24: بدان is a classical adverbial phrase ('with that' or 'thereby') formed from بـ + (آن) and here means 'by that/with them'. تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / until' and governs the following verb. سرآرد is a present/subjunctive-type narrative form 'may bring / come about' ('it may come to pass / reach'), بریشان = بر + ایشان 'upon them', and زمان here means 'time' or 'the right moment' — so the clause expresses purpose/intent: he prepared them so that when the time came it would (take effect) upon them.
segments.s586-g25: همی + verb is a poetic/habitual marker meaning a repeated or continuous action; برشکافید is a perfect/past-stem form meaning 'split/pierce open' (here used as the narrative past of action). پرشان is a possessive noun phrase 'their feathers' (پر + ـشان). به تیر is an instrumental phrase ('with/by arrow(s)') where به is used instrumentally.
segments.s586-g26: بدین سان is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in this manner' (ب- as the prepositional/prefixal element + دین/دینـ as part of بدین; سان = 'way/like'). زند is the verb زدن 'strikes/struck' (here a present/continuous or narrative action used after همی); مرد نخچیرگیر is a noun phrase 'the hunter-man' (مرد = man + نخچیرگیر = hunter) which follows the verb in poetic word order.
segments.s586-g27: به یک سوزن is a measure/instrumental phrase 'by one needle' (i.e., 'by even a needle's width'); این زان sets up a comparative ('this' vs. 'that' — این ... زان = 'this than that'); فزون‌تر نبود is the comparative predicate 'was not more' (فزون‌تر = 'more/greater', نبود = past negative 'was not').
segments.s586-g28: همان تیر is 'that very arrow' or 'the same arrow' as subject; زین تیر here functions as the comparative/benchmarked phrase ('than this arrow' — زین acting as an archaic/comparative particle), and برتر نبود is the predicate 'was not superior.' Word order is compact/poetic: subject + comparative phrase + predicate.
segments.s586-g29: برفت is a past verbal form 'he went off/left' (poetic past); و simply links actions ('and/then'); بدید is the past of دیدن 'he saw'; آنک is a relative/ demonstrative 'the one who/that which' introducing the following epithet بد نامدار ('ill-famed' — بد modifying نامدار), so the clause means 'he went and saw the one who was ill-famed.'
segments.s586-g30: به یک موی‌بر is an adverbial measure 'by a single hair' (مجازِ میزان = a hair's breadth); بود is the past copula 'was'; زخم سوار is a noun phrase 'the rider's wound' (زخم = wound, سوار = rider/horseman). The whole segment states the degree of the wound: it was only a hair's breadth.
segments.s586-g31: همی آفرین خواند is a repeated action: همی signals continuous/repeated exclamation and آفرین خواند = 'cried “bravo!”' (خواند here = 'called/exclaimed'). منذر is the proper name of the speaker placed after the verb (poetic word order). بدوی in this line appears as an epithet or linking word; its exact function/meaning is archaic/uncertain in this position.
segments.s586-g32: همان نیزه‌داران پرخاشجوی is a noun phrase 'those same spear-bearers, quarrelsome' (همان = that/same; نیزه‌داران = spear-bearers/lancers; پرخاشجوی = aggressive/quarrelsome). It is understood to act like the subject of the previous verb (i.e., they too shouted or expressed approval).
segments.s586-g1: This is a temporal/setting phrase: 'دگر هفته' = 'the next/another week' (دگر + هفته). 'با لشکری سرفراز' is a prepositional phrase 'with an army (لشکری) [that is] proud/victorious (سرفراز)'; adjective follows the noun in this poetic order.
segments.s586-g2: Main clause: 'به نخچیرگه رفت' = 'went to the hunting-ground' (به + place + past verb رفت). The following 'با یوز و باز' is an accompanying/instrumental phrase 'with cheetahs and falcons' that attaches to the verb (shows who/what accompanied him).
segments.s586-g3: 'برابر ز کوهی' is a locative phrase meaning 'opposite/by a mountain' (برابر = 'opposite/against', ز = poetic 'from/of', کوهی = 'mountain(ous)'). 'یکی شیر دید' is a typical narrative order: 'he saw a certain/a lion' (یکی = 'a certain/one', شیر = object 'lion', دید = past 'saw').
segments.s586-g4: Relative/locative clause: 'کجا' functions as 'where/there/which' referring back to the place; 'پشت' = 'back (of an animal)'; 'گوری' here refers to the animal gūr (onager) though that specific sense is not in the provided list (see senses); 'همی' is the habitual/continuous auxiliary showing ongoing action; 'بر درید' = 'tore at / rent open' (بر here is local/prefix, درید past of دریدن 'to tear').
segments.s586-g5: Clause: 'برآورد' is a past verb meaning 'raised/brought forth' in narrative (here 'raised' an outcry). 'زاغ سیه را' has the object marker 'را' on 'زاغ سیه' ('the black rook' as the thing brought/focalized). 'بزه' is an accusatory noun meaning 'fault/blame' (object or complement of برآورد).
segments.s586-g6: Adverbial + instrumental phrases before the verb: 'به تندی' = 'quickly/with haste' (به used adverbially), 'به شست' = 'with the thumb' (instrumental), 'سه‌پر' = 'three‑feathered' (modifier of the implement), and the verb 'زد' (past 'struck') comes at the end with its object 'گره' = 'knot'. Note the double 'به' marking adverbial/instrumental uses.
segments.s586-g7: 'دل گور' = 'the gūr's heart' (possessive noun + noun). 'بردوخت' here is a past verbal/adjectival form meaning 'was sewn/wounded/stitched' (poetically used of being torn or pierced). 'با پشت شیر' is an agent/instrument phrase 'by the lion's back' (با + پشت + شیر).
segments.s586-g8: 'پر از خون' = 'full of blood' (پر = 'full (of)'); 'هژبر' here is a proper name (Hezbar) whose blood is being described; the phrase 'از بر و گور زیر' strings prepositional pieces: 'از بر' = 'from the flank/side' and 'گور زیر' = 'under the gūr' (بر = 'on/above' and زیر = 'under'), so the line lists the places soaked with blood. The coordination is compact and typical of epic diction.
segments.s586-g41: بفرمود تا is a reporting/command clause: بفرمود (past, honorific) = 'he commanded/said', followed by تا + subordinate clause 'so that/that'. زخم او را is the definite direct object ('his wound' marked by را). به تیر is an instrumental/associative phrase ('with/by an arrow' or 'the arrow-wound'); here به functions to indicate the instrument/association.
segments.s586-g42: مصور نگاری کند is the core of the commanded subordinate clause: مصور ('the painter') + نگاری (a noun 'painting/portrait' used as the object/work) + کند (present/past-subjunctive/imperative sense 'make/do' used here to express 'let him have made' or 'cause to be made'). بر حریر is a locative phrase 'on silk' modifying نگاری. Word order (agent + object + verb + locative) is idiomatic in Persian poetry.
segments.s586-g43: سواری چو بهرام is a simile: سواری (rider/horseman) + چو ('like/as') + بهرام (proper name). با introduces a circumstantial phrase 'with ...' (با یال و کفت), coordinating two nouns with و. کفت here is poetic and used as 'foam/froth' describing the horse (hence sense 'foam').
segments.s586-g44: This short sequence piles up predicates/attributes: بلند ('tall') is an adjectival attribute; اشتری refers to 'a beast of burden' (a mount) and is presented as 'اشتری زیر' where زیر ('beneath/under') indicates the mount is under him (a poetic compressed order). و زخمی شگفت coordinates 'and' + زخمی ('wounded') modified by شگفت ('astonishing/wondrous') placed after the adjective as an intensifier — a common poetic order.
segments.s586-g45: This is a coordinated list describing the bow's ornamentation: کمان ('bow') followed by a noun phrase of items joined by و (مهره و شیر و آهو و گور). The nouns are listed as appositive details of the bow (beads, lion, gazelle, onager).
segments.s586-g46: گشاده is an adjective 'wide/open' modifying the preceding noun (the bow). بر here is the preposition 'on/upon' used in the poetic compressed phrase; the sequence 'و چربه دستی' links another attribute: چربه (literally 'grease' but idiomatically 'advantage/skill') + دستی ('hand', here 'skilled hand' or 'manual skill'). به زور is a prepositional phrase 'by force/with strength' modifying the action implied (common poetic compacting of agentive/ability features).
segments.s586-g47: A simple coordinated nominal series: شترمرغ و هامون و آن زخم تیر — 'the ostrich and the plain and that arrow-wound'. آن is the demonstrative 'that' qualifying زخم تیر ('the arrow-wound'). The sequence is the subject/topic of the following verb phrase.
segments.s586-g48: ز (poetic از) = 'from'; قیر سیه is a noun + adjective 'black pitch/tar' (قیر modified by سیه). تازه شد is a two-word verbal phrase 'became fresh/newly made' (شد = became, here auxiliary/predicative); بر حریر is the locative 'on silk' that completes the clause. Together they say these things were freshly rendered on the silk with black tar.

sentences.s586-s25: He let a horseman loose toward the king and sent that beloved close to him.
sentences.s586-s26: When the messenger arrived before Yazdegerd, the whole army gathered about that letter.
sentences.s586-s27: All the famous men remained behind, and they cried 'bravo' to Bahram.
sentences.s586-s28: And from that time, whenever you put your talents to use, they would continually rush against the king.
sentences.s586-s5: When he slew the onager and the brave lion, he strode into the iwan with a sword in his fist.
sentences.s586-s6: The following week Nuʿmān and Munḏir set out on the road, going with him to the hunting-ground.
sentences.s586-s7: He had taken many renowned (men) from the Arabs, from whom came ill ways in matters of profit and loss.
sentences.s586-s8: Munḏir wished that Bahram Gūr should give them horsemen and strength.
sentences.s586-s17: Munḏir said to him, "O sovereign, I rejoice for you like a rosebud when it bears (fruit)."
sentences.s586-s18: May it not be that your moon should bow; and if your loins/waist should grow weak...
sentences.s586-s19: At that very moment, as Munḏir reached the iwan, Bahram's report/counsel reached Keyvān.
sentences.s586-s20: Many painters sprang up (came running) from Yaman; these chiefs assembled at his gate in an assembly.
sentences.s586-s9: They saw an ostrich flock somewhere; they ran, each one like a wild beast loose.
sentences.s586-s10: When Bahram Gur saw that ostrich, he seized it up into the air as if like the wind.
sentences.s586-s11: He took up the bow, smiling in his hand like a talon, and struck four dagger-like arrows at the flank.
sentences.s586-s12: One by one he set them in the bow, so that when the time came it would be upon them.
sentences.s586-s13: He kept cleaving their feathers with arrows; in this way the hunter struck.
sentences.s586-s14: This was not greater than that by even a single needle; that same arrow was not superior to this arrow.
sentences.s586-s15: He went and saw the one ill-famed; the rider's wound was but the breadth of a single hair.
sentences.s586-s16: Munḏir kept crying 'Bravo!' — likewise the quarrelsome spear-bearers.
sentences.s586-s1: The following week, with a victorious army, he went to the hunting ground accompanied by cheetahs and falcons.
sentences.s586-s2: He saw a lion by a mountain; there it was tearing at the back of the gūr (onager).
sentences.s586-s3: A black rook raised an outcry of blame; swiftly, with his thumb, he struck the three‑feathered (thing) at the knot.
sentences.s586-s4: The onager's heart was rent by the lion's back; Hezbar's was full of blood, from the flank and beneath the onager.
sentences.s586-s21: He ordered that the painter portray his arrow-wound on silk.
sentences.s586-s22: A horseman like Bahram, with mane and foam, tall, with a beast of burden beneath him, and an astonishing wound.
sentences.s586-s23: A bow ornamented with beads and with lion, gazelle, and onager motifs — broad, and with a deft (greased) hand, by force.
sentences.s586-s24: The ostrich and the plain and that arrow-wound were newly made on the silk with black pitch.
